sources: LSAC Official Guide to ABA-Approved Law Schools (2025)

smart.label: Academic Rigor
what_counts: GPA (most heavily weighted single factor for law school admissions)
LSAT score (equally weighted with GPA at most schools)
Writing-intensive and analytical coursework (philosophy, logic, political theory, history)
what_does_not_count: Padded coursework or GPA inflation from easy majors without depth
GPA without LSAT mention - both must be present for law school
Certifications unrelated to analytical or writing skill

grit.label: Leadership and Persistence
what_counts: Sustained leadership in organizations (multi-year, not just a title)
Demonstrated ownership and outcomes in roles
Commitment to a cause, community, or goal over time
Work experience that shows discipline and responsibility
what_does_not_count: Mock trial or debate - not required and should not be flagged as a gap
One-semester leadership roles with no evidence of impact

build.label: Legal-Adjacent Proof
what_counts: Legal internship (law firm, public defender, DA office, legal aid clinic)
Writing sample (published article, thesis, policy memo, legal brief)
Research with a professor in law-adjacent field
what_does_not_count: General internships with no legal or analytical component
Club memberships without leadership or output

common_gaps.gap: No LSAT score or prep signal on resume
common_gaps.fix: LSAT is the single most important metric. If not yet taken, note prep timeline. A 170+ opens every T14 door.

common_gaps.gap: GPA below 3.7 with no upward trend narrative
common_gaps.fix: An addendum explaining GPA dip (semester difficulty, personal circumstance, upward trend) is standard practice and effective.
Even one semester at a legal aid clinic, public defender's office, or policy shop significantly strengthens your application.

common_gaps.gap: No writing sample or analytical writing artifact
common_gaps.fix: Law schools want proof of your writing ability before you arrive. A thesis, published article, or policy memo is essential.

common_gaps.gap: Shallow extracurricular leadership (title only, no outcomes)
common_gaps.fix: Add one concrete outcome per leadership role: members recruited, budget managed, event impact, policy change driven.

common_gaps.gap: No 'why law' narrative connecting undergrad to legal ambitions
common_gaps.fix: Adcoms want to see how your undergraduate experiences pointed you toward law. A clear through-line across your resume matters.

common_gaps.gap: No coursework in legal theory, political philosophy, or policy
common_gaps.fix: Add a relevant course or two to your resume header or transcript narrative: constitutional law, jurisprudence, policy analysis.

schools.name: Yale Law School
priorities: Writing quality (personal statement is heavily weighted)
schools.notes: Most holistic T14. Lower LSAT candidates (169-171) can be admitted with exceptional writing, public interest background, or academic distinction. Writing sample and personal statement carry enormous weight here more than anywhere else.

schools.name: Harvard Law School
schools.notes: More numbers-focused than Yale. Leadership with scale or real-world impact is a major differentiator. Strong preference for candidates who have done something, not just studied.

schools.name: Stanford Law School
schools.notes: Smaller class than HLS/YLS (about 180/year) makes it intensely selective. Entrepreneurial backgrounds and candidates who have built something stand out. Personal statement authenticity is critical.

schools.name: Columbia Law School
priorities: Biglaw and corporate law career interest
schools.notes: GPA range is wider than peers - a high LSAT can compensate significantly. Best BigLaw placement in NYC. Strong preference for candidates interested in transactional or corporate law.

schools.name: University of Chicago Law School
schools.notes: The 'why UChicago' essay matters a lot. They want people who love ideas and can argue. Law-and-economics tradition means quant skills and economics/philosophy backgrounds stand out.

schools.name: NYU School of Law
schools.notes: Top public interest law school in the country. Root-Tilden-Kern scholarship covers full tuition for public interest commitment. Strong international law program. More holistic than Columbia.

schools.name: University of Pennsylvania Carey Law School
priorities: Business and corporate law alignment (Wharton proximity)
schools.notes: JD/MBA pipeline with Wharton is a unique draw. Business-minded pre-law students are a natural fit. Work experience before law school is viewed positively.

schools.name: University of Virginia School of Law
schools.notes: Known for its collegial culture. Character matters more here than at pure numbers-focused schools. Strong federal clerkship placement. Southern law market stronghold.

schools.name: University of Michigan Law School
schools.notes: GPA spread is wide - a strong narrative can compensate for a lower GPA. Diversity is genuinely prioritized. Strong regional presence in Midwest/Great Lakes.

schools.name: Duke University School of Law
schools.notes: Strong international law and business law programs. GPA range is wide. Scholarship opportunities are competitive - high LSAT earns significant funding.

schools.name: Northwestern Pritzker School of Law
schools.notes: Most work-experience-friendly of the T14. Actively prefers applicants who have worked for a few years. Average age of entering class is higher than peers. Chicago BigLaw pipeline.

schools.name: Cornell Law School
schools.notes: Most accessible T14 by LSAT. Strong international and comparative law. Ithaca location means concentrated community culture - fit matters more than at NYC/DC schools.

schools.name: Georgetown University Law Center
priorities: Diversity and access to legal education
schools.notes: Best DC legal market placement. Huge class (~550/year) means more holistic review - softs matter more here. Public interest and government candidates thrive. Most accessible T14 entry point.

schools.name: UCLA School of Law
schools.notes: Top California law school. Strong in entertainment, IP, and public interest. California bar alignment makes it dominant in the LA/SF legal market.
